topic: An artist's work is displayed without their permission
raw_output:
[story 1]

The gallery lights felt too bright, a harsh, unforgiving glare on the canvas. Elias stood near the entrance, arms crossed tight over his chest, his gaze sweeping over the assembled crowd with an air of practiced detachment. His latest collection, the one he’d poured six months of sleepless nights into, hung on the walls—vibrant, chaotic, undeniably *his*. A woman with a knowing smile leaned toward a critic, gesturing toward his work with an easy confidence that grated on Elias’s nerves. He shifted his weight, the leather of his jacket creaking softly. *This is absurd,* he thought, the word a cold knot in his stomach. He took a slow, deliberate breath, cataloging every subtle shift in the room, searching for the moment the facade would crack, the moment he’d realize this wasn't a display; it was a theft.

[story 2]

I watched the installation from across the room, a faint tremor running through my hands. The colors screamed defiance, the textures begged to be touched, and they were all undeniably mine. Yet, the way they were positioned—the deliberate framing, the curated lighting—felt invasive. A murmur rippled through the onlookers, appreciative and loud. I pressed my palms against my thighs, trying to anchor myself against the rising tide of unease. Someone had orchestrated this. I scanned the perimeter, my eyes darting from the security cameras to the faces of the patrons, searching for the architect of this unauthorized exhibition. A tight, humorless smile touched my lips as I forced myself to move toward the exit, needing air that didn't smell of expensive varnish and misplaced ownership.

[story 3]

The silence in the room was deafening, punctuated only by the soft rustle of expensive fabric. I leaned against a marble pillar, my posture rigid, every muscle coiled. The paintings were arresting, undeniably skillful, but the context was wrong. They were displayed without a single introductory statement, without any acknowledgment of the creator. A patron nearby pointed, their voice high-pitched with admiration. I didn't look at them. My focus narrowed, fixed on the edges of the frame, calculating angles, tracing the invisible lines of intrusion. A slow burn started in my chest, a deep, unsettling heat that made my jaw clench. I straightened up, a slow, deliberate movement, and began to walk toward the centerpiece, my footsteps echoing too loudly in the cavernous space.
